title: Five (+ one) Killer Works of Horror to Read in October 2020
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZA_G9BP9HLY

With a game still bedding in on the table, Daniel (@dungeondive) turns to books, offering five works of horror fiction (plus one bonus) to read in October 2020. First is Thomas Ligotti's My Work Is Not Yet Done, corporate horror in which office anxiety literally thickens into fog — Daniel rates the two shorter pieces, including "I Have a Special Plan for This World," above the title novella, and points listeners to Current 93's terrifying reading of the source poem. Then comes the real discovery: Rikki Ducornet's The Complete Butcher's Tales, over fifty stories in 172 pages, from which he reads two complete miniatures of pure nightmare fuel. He calls her one of the most unsettling writers he's ever encountered and vows to read her entire catalogue. Robert W. Chambers's The King in Yellow follows — home to "The Repairer of Reputations," possibly Daniel's favourite short story of all time, a work that "feels dangerous" to read — with the Starry Wisdom Library auction-catalogue as the bonus volume. Two anthologies close the list: Douglas E. Winter's Prime Evil, starring a masterpiece of painterly madness from Rambo creator David Morrell, and the ubiquitous Barnes & Noble bargain-bin gem To Sleep, Perchance to Dream, which Daniel crowns the all-time great horror anthology.

----

Daniel's sleeper pick is a Barnes & Noble house anthology you can find for pennies in used bookstores. What's the best horror book you've ever pulled from a bargain bin — the one nobody talks about but everyone should own?
